summary:
The Han dynasty extensions of the Great Wall (c. 121–101 BC, under Emperor Wu) significantly expanded and strengthened the Qin-era walls that formed the original [[event:1121|Great Wall of China Qin]] construction, pushing the fortifications westward through the Hexi Corridor (Gansu) toward Central Asia. The Han extensions were built partly to protect the newly conquered western territories and to secure the [[event:1129|Silk Road Establishment]] trade route from Xiongnu raiders.

Han wall construction differed from the Qin version in its use of locally available materials — in the Gobi Desert regions, walls were built from compressed layers of tamarisk branches, gravel, and sand rather than stone, traces of which still survive. The walls were integrated with a sophisticated signaling system of watchtowers (beacon towers) spaced so that fire or smoke signals could relay messages across hundreds of kilometers within hours. Garrison troops cultivated fields adjacent to the wall for supply self-sufficiency. Archaeological discoveries of Han-era wooden slips (documents) from these Gobi outposts have provided extraordinary information about daily military life, administrative procedures, and the practical realities of frontier defense on China's northwestern border.
